variables.content: The computed quasiparticle effective mass m* for paramagnetic 2D-UEL exhibits a systematic dependence on the simulation-cell electron number N that is empirically well described by the form m*(N) = m*(∞) + β·N^{-3/2} for some constant β; applying this N^{-3/2} empirical scaling to finite-N VMC and DMC data (for example N = 146, 218, 302) permits controlled extrapolation to estimates of m*(∞) in the thermodynamic limit. Observationally in the reported datasets, finite-size errors of m* are smaller in VMC than in DMC and the finite-size errors grow as the density is reduced (larger r_s).

For a two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas (2D HEG) simulated in a periodically repeated finite simulation cell containing N electrons, let E_N denote the computed energy per electron of that N-electron finite system and let E_∞ denote the energy per electron in the thermodynamic limit (infinite system size). The proposition is: the dominant many-body finite-size bias in the energy per electron for the fluid phase falls off for large N as a single leading asymptotic term proportional to N^{-5/4}, i.e., E_N = E_∞ - c N^{-5/4} + o(N^{-5/4}) for some constant c, and furthermore this asymptotic N^{-5/4} dependence provides a quantitatively valid description for the finite system sizes employed in the reported calculations (specifically for system sizes up to about N = 162), so that fitting finite-N results to that leading form yields a meaningful extrapolation to E_∞.
      N.D. Drummond et al., Phys. Rev. B 78, 125106 (2008)

The 2D calculations assume an ideal strictly two-dimensional electron gas with the bare Coulomb interaction $v_{q}=2\pi e^{2}/q$ and therefore omit finite quasi-2D layer-width form-factor corrections that, for a realistic quasi-2D quantum well or heterostructure, modify the effective interaction at large wavevectors $q$. The explicitly stated quantitative effect of such finite-width form factors is to reduce the 2D many-body renormalization by factors on the order of roughly 1.2–2 depending on carrier density and confinement details; the weak point is the assumption that omitting these finite-width form factors does not change the qualitative conclusions about the spin-polarization dependence of the effective mass (majority mass decreasing with $\zeta$, minority mass nonmonotonic).
      [14]

The two-dimensional electron-gas contribution to the normal induction jump at $z=a$ is represented by a local surface-plasma term parameterized by the scalar two-dimensional plasma frequency
      $$
      \omega_s^2(\aleph)=\frac{4\pi e^2 n_{os}\aleph}{m_s},
      $$
      where $e$ is the electron charge, $n_{os}$ is the equilibrium 2D surface density, $m_s$ is the effective 2D mass; in deriving this local form one neglects nonlocal (wavevector-dependent beyond the explicit $\aleph$ prefactor), finite-thickness, and quantum-size corrections to the 2D response.

For the homogeneous unpolarized two-dimensional electron gas in a finite square cell, adding one electron with momentum \mathbf{p} (|\mathbf{p}|>k_F) to the N-particle ground state produces a finite-size correction to the total kinetic-energy contribution of the excitation given by
      \delta T_{\mathbf{p}}^{N}=\frac{\hbar^{2}\mathbf{p}^{2}}{2m}\;Z_{\mathbf{p}}^{N}\big[e^{-\Delta_{N}}-1\big],
      where Z_{\mathbf{p}}^{N} is the finite-N quasiparticle weight at momentum \mathbf{p} and \Delta_{N} is the missing-small-q integral defined in conclusion 1. Because \Delta_{N}\sim N^{-1/4} for large N, the leading finite-size error in the near-k_F excitation dispersion used to extract the single-particle effective mass m^{*} (via the linearized relation \varepsilon_{\mathbf{k}}\approx\hbar^{2}k_F(k-k_F)/m^{*}) also scales as N^{-1/4}; therefore, extrapolations that assume faster vanishing finite-size errors can give qualitatively incorrect conclusions about the sign and magnitude of m^{*}-m for typical accessible system sizes.

For finite-system variational Monte Carlo studies of the two-dimensional electron gas in the density range $1\le r_{s}\le5$ and system sizes tested ($N=26,58,98$), extracting the effective mass $m^{*}$ by (i) computing correlated energy differences among excited states at fixed particle number and (ii) fitting those differences to the angular expansion for $\Delta E_{\alpha\beta}$ to obtain $f_{1}^{s}$ and then $m^{*}$ via $m^{*}/m=(1-\tfrac{1}{4}r_{s}^{2}N f_{1}^{s})^{-1}$ produces results with much smaller and more systematic finite-size dependence than extracting $m^{*}$ from energy differences between ground and excited states combined with external compressibility and susceptibility inputs; this conclusion is supported by the observed near-independence of the fitted combinations $N f_{l}$ across the tested $N$ values when the excited-state-difference method is used.

For the two-dimensional uniform electron liquid (2D-UEL) model consisting of N interacting electrons of charge −e and bare mass m in a finite periodic simulation cell of area A (so that the density is ρ = N/A) and with density parameter r_s = (1/(πρ))^{1/2}a_B^{-1} (using atomic units with Bohr radius a_B = 1), the finite-size dependence of the quasiparticle effective mass m*(N) obtained from quantum Monte Carlo band-derivative estimates is taken to follow the empirical form
      m*(N) = m*(∞) + β·N^{-3/2}
      for some constant β, and this empirical scaling relation is applied to the authors' simulation data obtained at N = 146, 218, and 302 electrons to extrapolate m*(∞) for densities in the metallic range 1 ≤ r_s ≤ 5.
      Holzmann et al., Theory of finite size effects for electronic quantum Monte Carlo calculations of liquids and solids, Phys. Rev. B 94, 035126 (2016).

The finite-temperature GW-RPA on-shell calculations reproduce, in the $T\to0$ limit, the established zero-temperature RPA effective-mass results obtained in prior RPA studies of the two-dimensional electron gas, and the numerical extrapolation of the present finite-temperature results to the extreme low-temperature limit yields an effective-mass enhancement as large as $m^{*}/m\sim5$ for dilute two-dimensional electron systems at interaction strength $r_{s}\approx10$, where $r_{s}=me^{2}/(\hbar^{2}\sqrt{\pi n})$.

For the fully spin-polarized two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas at density parameter r_s=10, extrapolation of finite-N DMC quartic-fit effective masses yields the thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass m^*(∞)=0.70(3) a.u., where m^*=k_F/(dE/dk)_{k_F} for the ferromagnetic Fermi wave vector.

For a sequence of computed finite-size quasiparticle effective masses \\(m^{*}(N)\\) obtained by DMC for a 2D HEG at fixed density and spin polarization over the available range of system sizes \\(N\\), the proposition is: the dependence of \\(m^{*}(N)\\) on \\(N\\) can be modelled to good approximation by a single-term power-law finite-size correction,
      $$
      m^{*}(N)=m^{*}(\\infty)+b\\,N^{-\\gamma},
      $$
      with approximately constant parameters \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\), \\(b\\), and exponent \\(\\gamma\\) across the sampled \\(N\\)-range, so that this parametric form provides a valid basis for extrapolation to the thermodynamic limit within the statistical precision of the DMC data.
